And in fact, the OS has to sort of have modifications. In the case of Linux, it has basically three things. It has namespaces and control groups and a union file system. So namespaces allow you to isolate one container

from another. Control groups allow you to control the resource footprint a container would have. And the union file system allows you to mount and share files across containers and across the host. So Linux namespaces gives a process its own view of identifiers separate from other processes or other containers. So when you create a process, it normally, of course, shares the identifiers, shares with every process or anything on that system. But you can use an unshare command which can detach a child from its parents' namespace. And then you can what's called clone different types of namespaces to allow that child to specify its own namespaces. And it can do that for process IDs, it could have its own mount points, it could have its own network devices, its own user and group IDs and so forth. So you can completely change the namespace such that it's not visible outside this particular Linux process or container or outside of any of those processes within a container. And then the question is, well, what can a container use resource-wise if we're now going to virtualize things, we're going to have many containers, we need to be able to constrain kind of what they are able to do. and the way that's done in Linux is with a C group. So basically what C groups are, they're these defined resource allocation templates, if you will. So let's take memory, for example. So under C group memory, you might have different subgroups, say A and B. A sets the memory limit to be a gigabyte. B might set the memory limit to be two gigabytes. Another might be PIDs, which maybe there's template B in which sets the maximum number you can use as a thousand and so forth. And so there's a rich hierarchy of C-groups already provided for you. We've talked about a couple of these before. but I want to put them all together. So a server can be stateful. This means the client maintains persistent client state. So if it were to crash and get restarted, it will remember the client that it's interacting with, that it interacted with during that crash. It will remember information in the flow between the client and that server, between even separate requests. It will hold that state. We gave an example of suppose the image server held the matrix and the client was operating from that matrix, for example. We could have a stateless server where the server has no client state or maybe transient client state. And if it had any state, it might be a hint. Okay. So, you know, we have the example here where maybe the client has to send the matrix that's been modified or that, you know, he wants to apply to the next operator. The server is stateless in the sense that it doesn't hold any memory of what the client's doing. It just gets its matrix and it lets the operator and sends it back and doesn't remember the matrix.
